needs_verification.note: Brand page lists 215g for 50cm without specifying spike material batch; confirm against a review's measured weight before relying on it for a hard cutoff.

review_result.summary: Reviewers consistently praise the Aero Lite's weight, calling it one of the lightest true ice axes on the market and a natural pick for glacier travel and ski mountaineering where the axe mostly rides on a pack. Durability draws more mixed comments: the aluminum shaft and head hold up fine for glacier travel and low-angle snow but several reviewers caution against sustained technical ice or mixed use, where the pick is noted to dull and deform faster than steel-headed axes. Reviewers rate the swing and self-arrest performance as adequate but not confidence-inspiring for anything steeper than moderate snow slopes.

review_links.key_takeaway: Excellent weight-to-utility ratio for glacier travel; not built for technical ice.
Durable enough for repeated ski mountaineering seasons, but the aluminum pick dulls quickly on hard ice.

ambiguities: Petzl also sells a non-Light 'Laser Speed' model with a heavier steel shaft; resolved to the Light version based on the candidate name.

review_result.summary: Reviewers weighted toward technical mountaineering rate the Laser Speed Light highly for its swing and pick bite on hard ice and mixed terrain, well above what an ultralight glacier axe like the Aero Lite offers. The tradeoff is weight and price: reviewers note it's noticeably heavier on the pack for approaches that don't need technical performance, and the interchangeable pick system adds long-term cost. Build quality and steel head durability get uniformly positive marks even after multiple seasons of technical use.

review_links.key_takeaway: Top-tier technical performance; heavier than pure glacier-travel axes.
Steel head holds an edge through repeated technical seasons with minimal wear.

run_notes: Both candidates resolved via official brand product pages.
Petzl Laser Speed Light exceeds the 250g weight requirement (330g); flagged as a requirement violation rather than dropped, per v1 policy.
